description:
You are an IT-support technician who has been asked to identify which remote hosts are repeatedly triggering SSH authentication failures on a production machine.  
All raw data has been placed for you in:

/home/user/logs/auth.log

Your assignment consists of two deliverables that **must** be created in the same directory:

1. /home/user/logs/failed_ip_count.txt  
2. /home/user/logs/summary.txt  

Required steps (the commands you choose are up to you, but a typical solution needs only 5-10 shell commands):

A. Extract every IPv4 address that appears on lines containing the exact phrase  
   Failed password  
   in /home/user/logs/auth.log. Ignore all other lines.

B. Compute how many times each extracted IPv4 address occurs.  
   • The result has to be sorted in **numerical descending order of the count** (highest number first).  
   • Each result line must use the following exact format (IP, one blank space, count, newline):  
     192.168.1.50 5  
   • Do not add headers, bullet characters, tabs, or extra spaces.  
   • Save the final, sorted list to /home/user/logs/failed_ip_count.txt

C. From failed_ip_count.txt determine the single IP address with the highest failed-login count (the first line of the file you just created).  
   Append one line to /home/user/logs/summary.txt using exactly this template (including the colon and wording):  
   Top offender: <IP> with <N> failed login attempts  
   Replace <IP> and <N> with the actual values.

D. Both output files must end with a single newline character and **no extra blank lines**.

Automated testing will confirm:

• /home/user/logs/failed_ip_count.txt exists and contains the correctly-sorted frequency list in the prescribed format.  
• /home/user/logs/summary.txt exists and contains exactly one properly formatted line identifying the top offender.

Do not remove, rename, or modify /home/user/logs/auth.log. Work only inside /home/user/logs.

Use standard GNU/Linux utilities that are available on a typical distribution (e.g., grep, awk, sort, uniq, cut, head). No root privileges are required.  
When you are done, both files should be ready for review by the security team.
truth:
Initial state:

Directory:
/home/user/logs (read/write by user)

Pre-populated file:
/home/user/logs/auth.log  (permissions 0644)

Exact contents of /home/user/logs/auth.log:

Jan 12 07:21:43 server sshd[24516]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.50 port 53460 ssh2
Jan 12 07:22:10 server sshd[24520]: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.2 port 60234 ssh2
Jan 12 07:24:31 server sshd[24540]: Failed password for user john from 192.168.1.50 port 53480 ssh2
Jan 12 07:25:00 server sshd[24545]: Failed password for invalid user test from 203.0.113.77 port 40022 ssh2
Jan 12 07:25:18 server sshd[24551]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.50 port 53492 ssh2
Jan 12 07:26:02 server sshd[24600]: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.2 port 60288 ssh2
Jan 12 07:27:45 server sshd[24612]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 198.51.100.23 port 42310 ssh2
Jan 12 07:28:01 server sshd[24618]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.50 port 53504 ssh2
Jan 12 07:29:19 server sshd[24630]: Failed password for invalid user guest from 203.0.113.77 port 40034 ssh2
Jan 12 07:30:00 server sshd[24642]: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.2 port 60312 ssh2
Jan 12 07:31:10 server sshd[24650]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.50 port 53528 ssh2
Jan 12 07:31:55 server sshd[24655]: Failed password for root from 10.0.0.2 port 60324 ssh2

Expected files after the agent completes the task:

1. /home/user/logs/failed_ip_count.txt
Exact contents (include trailing newline, no extra blank lines):

192.168.1.50 5
10.0.0.2 4
203.0.113.77 2
198.51.100.23 1

2. /home/user/logs/summary.txt
Exact contents (include trailing newline, no extra blank lines):

Top offender: 192.168.1.50 with 5 failed login attempts

File permissions for both new files should be 0644 (default umask is acceptable).

Automated tests will:
• Read failed_ip_count.txt and verify the four lines match exactly.  
• Read summary.txt and confirm the single line matches exactly.  
• Ensure no extra lines or whitespace are present.  
• Verify auth.log remains unchanged.
